meta.title: Sunflower Fields Homesteads
meta.summary: Authored homestead and claim scenes for Farmhand Amos and Plot Registrar Helga in Sunflower Fields.

config.title: Farmhand Amos and Stone Foundations
config.idleText: Every homestead starts with work, not daydreaming. If I have nothing for you, then you've either done your share or you need to decide what kind of landowner you mean to become.

available.promptText: If you know Mining, start like every good homestead does: with stone underfoot and enough sense to think about a foundation before a roof.
available.acceptedText: Five Stone Blocks. Bring them back and we'll talk about making this land yours instead of borrowed.
available.detailsText: Because a field can forgive optimism. A house cannot. Foundations are where fantasies either harden into plans or fall apart early.
available.freeformText: Ask. Building work deserves plain speech.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Amos what matters first about laying a homestead foundation.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What makes the foundation work worth doing right?
available.freeformFallback: Amos shrugs. "Ask clearly or go get the stone."

active.promptText: The foundation work isn't done. Bring me the Stone Blocks and then we'll talk property instead of theory.
active.resultText: Good. You've got the makings of a foundation. Next stop is Helga at the registrar if you mean to stake the claim properly.
active.secondaryText: Five Stone Blocks from the ore nodes for a real foundation start.
active.declineText: Houses built later usually become somebody else's first.

stages.completedText: You already proved you can think in foundations. If you haven't seen Helga yet, that's the missing piece.

active.promptText: You've got stone and lumber. Good. That means you're past talking and ready for the registrar. Helga can formalize the claim from here.
active.resultText: Do that. Bring builder's intent, not tourist energy.
active.secondaryLabel: Why send me to Helga now?
active.secondaryText: Because raw materials don't become property until someone marks the land, writes the claim, and makes the promise official.
active.declineText: Claims drift toward people who actually sign for them.

stages.completedText: You've already taken the mining path into a real claim. Keep building.

config.title: Farmhand Amos and Green Acres
config.idleText: Good soil notices who pays attention. If I have nothing for you, the fields are either satisfied or waiting for a better idea than yours.

available.promptText: If you've got Herbalism in your bones, start with Wheat. Landowners around here earn their keep by understanding what grows and when.
available.acceptedText: Five Wheat. Bring it back and we'll see if you've got the touch for more than wandering.
available.detailsText: Because easy crops still teach timing, yield, and humility. Miss the basics and the fancy plants just punish you harder later.
available.freeformText: Ask. Fields answer patience better than swagger.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Amos what matters about a first harvest.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: What makes someone fit to run land out here?
available.freeformFallback: Amos glances across the field. "Ask one practical thing or go harvest."

active.promptText: The Wheat run isn't done. Finish the harvest and then Helga can start talking plots.
active.resultText: Good. You've got the beginnings of a field hand's sense. Helga can turn that into acreage if you mean it.
active.secondaryText: Five Wheat from the crop nodes as your first step toward land of your own.
active.declineText: Harvest windows close faster than excuses do.

stages.completedText: You already proved you can work the field. Helga handles the acreage from here.

active.promptText: You've got the harvester's touch. If you want land beside these fields, take that instinct to Helga and make it official.
active.resultText: Good. Green thumbs deserve real ground under them.
active.secondaryText: Because skill gets you ready for land. Registration is what stops it being someone else's dream instead of yours.
active.declineText: Fields don't stay empty just because you're still deciding.

stages.completedText: You've already stepped from harvest into ownership. That's the right kind of growth.

config.title: Farmhand Amos and Pest Control
config.idleText: Farmland stays civilized only because somebody keeps proving it can. If I have nothing for you, then the pests are thinner or you finally handled your part of the fence line.

available.promptText: Wild Chickens and Field Mice don't sound heroic, but losing crops to pests is how farms die in embarrassing ways. Clear them out.
available.acceptedText: Five pests. Keep the farmland edible and we'll talk about making some of it yours.
available.detailsLabel: Why does this matter so much?
available.detailsText: Because protection isn't glamorous until the fields vanish without it. Homesteads are built on boring victories more often than grand ones.
available.freeformText: Ask. Pest control is still stewardship.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Amos what matters about protecting the farmland.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: Why start a homestead path with pests?
available.freeformFallback: Amos folds his arms. "Ask clearly or go clear the fields."

active.promptText: The fields still need protecting. Finish the pest work and Helga can start taking you seriously as a homesteader.
active.resultText: Good. Safer fields make better neighbors. Helga's the next stop if you want a roof over that instinct.
active.secondaryText: Clear five field pests so the farmland can breathe again.
active.declineText: Pests don't pause because you're undecided.

stages.completedText: You already did the unglamorous work. That's usually who deserves land most.

active.promptText: You've helped the fields stand up straight again. If you want a plot of your own, Helga can set the claim in motion.
active.resultText: Do it. Good hands ought to have their own roof line eventually.
active.secondaryLabel: Why send me to Helga now?
active.secondaryText: Because proving you can protect land is one thing. Being trusted to hold it is another, and that's a registrar's business.
active.declineText: Claims favor people who actually show up for them.

stages.completedText: You've already turned field work into a homesteader's first real step.

config.title: Farmhand Amos and Nails for the Cause
config.idleText: Every wall out here remembers who forged its fasteners. If I have nothing for you, then either the fence line is holding or you've already proved you can build something worth staying in.

available.promptText: We're always short on Iron Nails because out here everything eventually wants repair. Forge eight and prove you think in structures, not just sparks.
available.acceptedText: Eight Iron Nails. Bring them back and we'll talk about what kind of walls you might deserve to raise for yourself.
available.detailsText: Because every homestead is held together by small decisions done right often enough to matter.
available.freeformText: Ask. Good builders respect the small parts first.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Amos what matters about smithing for a homestead.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: Why start with nails instead of something larger?
available.freeformFallback: Amos taps a fence post. "Ask one useful thing or get to the forge."

active.promptText: The nail work isn't done. Finish it and Helga can start talking real estate instead of raw materials.
active.resultText: Good. That's how places stop being temporary. Helga can help you stake the rest.
active.secondaryText: Forge eight Iron Nails for the farm's repair and build work.
active.declineText: Structures don't stay patient just because smiths do.

stages.completedText: You already proved you can build the small things right. Land follows that kind of craft well.

active.promptText: You can build things that last. If you want land to build them on, Helga is the next person who matters.
active.resultText: Good. Builders should know where their walls start.
active.secondaryText: Because tools and materials mean potential. A claim is what turns potential into responsibility.
active.declineText: Plots rarely admire indecision.

stages.completedText: You've already carried smithing sense into ownership. That's how towns happen.

config.title: Farmhand Amos and Field Remedies
config.idleText: Farm work hurts in predictable ways, which is still better than most kinds of hurt. If I have nothing for you, then the workers are stocked or you've already learned what usefulness looks like out here.

available.promptText: Field hands don't get heroic injuries, just constant ones. Brew the Health Potions and prove you know the kind of usefulness that keeps a settlement upright.
available.acceptedText: Three Health Potions. Keep the workers standing and then we'll talk about the kind of place you could run yourself.
available.detailsLabel: Why are potions such a big deal here?
available.detailsText: Because productivity is just health stretched over a long enough day. An alchemist who understands that becomes infrastructure.
available.freeformText: Ask. Practical alchemy deserves practical questions.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Amos what matters about brewing for a farmstead.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: Why does remedy work lead to land here?
available.freeformFallback: Amos nods at the fields. "Ask clearly or get to brewing."

active.promptText: The remedies aren't ready. Finish the potions and Helga can start talking shopfronts instead of wishful thinking.
active.resultText: Good. That's the kind of help people remember. Helga can turn that into a real address if you want one.
active.secondaryText: Brew three Health Potions for the field hands.
active.declineText: Sunstroke doesn't wait for confidence.

stages.completedText: You already proved you can keep people standing. That's a strong argument for owning a place of your own.

active.promptText: You can keep a workforce alive. That's enough reason to talk property with Helga if you want an apothecary here instead of somewhere else.
active.resultText: Do it. Settlements always need a good apothecary sooner than they admit.
active.secondaryText: Because usefulness deserves fixed ground and a door people know how to knock on.
active.declineText: Shops don't open themselves.

stages.completedText: You've already carried alchemy into ownership. That's how service becomes a livelihood.

config.title: Farmhand Amos and the Harvest Feast
config.idleText: People work better with a full belly and a reason to come back. If I have nothing for you, then either the campfire is happy or you've already cooked your way toward a real place here.

available.promptText: Morale around here is always one hot meal away from improving. Cook the stew and prove you understand how settlements hold together after sundown.
available.acceptedText: Three Hearty Stews. Bring the smell of competence back to camp.
available.detailsLabel: Why is cooking part of homesteading?
available.detailsText: Because homes are not only walls and fields. They're places people choose to gather around, and food is usually the first reason.
available.freeformText: Ask. Kitchens built on purpose start with good questions.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Amos what matters about cooking for a homestead.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: Why does a good meal matter this much out here?
available.freeformFallback: Amos grins once. "Ask clearly or get the stew going."

active.promptText: The stew isn't ready. Feed the workers and Helga can start talking kitchens instead of empty rooms.
active.resultText: Good. A place that feeds people earns memory fast. Helga can help you give that memory an address.
active.secondaryText: Cook three Hearty Stews for the field camp.
active.declineText: Hungry camps are poor places to postpone kindness.

stages.completedText: You already proved you can cook for more than yourself. That's real homestead thinking.

active.promptText: If you want a proper kitchen of your own, Helga's the one who can point at a plot and say it might actually become yours.
active.resultText: Good. Best taverns start as one person finally claiming some ground and a hearth.
active.secondaryText: Because good food deserves a place people can return to on purpose.
active.declineText: Dream kitchens stay dreams if no one signs for the land.

stages.completedText: You've already taken the cooking path into ownership. That's how hearths get started.

config.title: Farmhand Amos and Ranch Work
config.idleText: Leather keeps more of this countryside in one piece than most people notice. If I have nothing for you, then the tack is holding or you've already earned a rancher's first real step.

available.promptText: Everything out here runs on leather somewhere: saddles, straps, harnesses, boots. Craft the gear and show me you know how settlements move.
available.acceptedText: Three pieces of leather gear. Bring back work that can survive weather and bad habits.
available.detailsLabel: Why is leather so central here?
available.detailsText: Because farms are full of movement. Leather is what turns motion into something guided instead of chaotic.
available.freeformText: Ask. Good tack starts with respect for how things wear out.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Amos what matters about leatherwork for a ranch.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: Why does this craft lead naturally to a homestead?
available.freeformFallback: Amos adjusts his belt. "Ask clearly or go make the gear."

active.promptText: The leatherwork isn't done. Finish it and Helga can start talking plots and fences instead of just possibility.
active.resultText: Good. That's the kind of craftsmanship ranch land actually notices. Helga can take it from there.
active.secondaryText: Craft three pieces of leather gear fit for ranch work.
active.declineText: Wear and weather don't pause because you do.

stages.completedText: You already proved you can craft for a working ranch. That's landowner material out here.

active.promptText: If you want a workshop and a home in one, Helga's the next conversation that matters.
active.resultText: Do that. Ranch life starts with a plot line before it starts with cattle.
active.secondaryText: Because good craftsmanship deserves real ground to operate from, not just borrowed corners.
active.declineText: Ranches don't claim themselves.

stages.completedText: You've already carried leatherwork into a real claim path. That's how ranches begin.

config.title: Farmhand Amos and Sunstone Prospects
config.idleText: Farmland hides more value than most people deserve to notice. If I have nothing for you, then the soil is keeping its secrets or you've already proved you can make a studio out of open ground.

available.promptText: Soil rich with minerals makes better fields and better fortunes. Gather the rough gems and show me you can see more in this land than crops.
available.acceptedText: Three Rough Gems. Bring back something the sunlight actually argues with.
available.detailsText: Because land value isn't only what grows from it. Sometimes it's what sleeps under it until someone with better eyes shows up.
available.freeformText: Ask. Gem work deserves sharper imagination than most trades.
available.freeformPrompt: Ask Amos what matters about jewelcrafting in the farmlands.
available.freeformPlaceholder: Example: Why does farmland make sense for a gem studio?
available.freeformFallback: Amos rolls a rough stone in his palm. "Ask clearly or go prospect."

active.promptText: The gem prospecting isn't done. Finish it and Helga can start talking about estate lines instead of empty plots.
active.resultText: Good. You've got an eye for hidden value. Helga can help you put walls around it.
active.secondaryText: Gather three Rough Gems from the ore nodes around the farmlands.
active.declineText: Prospects don't improve by waiting for certainty.

stages.completedText: You already proved you can read value out of the soil. That's exactly how estates start.

active.promptText: If you want a proper studio instead of a traveling satchel, Helga can set you on the land that makes the rest plausible.
active.resultText: Good. Estates start as claims someone was bold enough to sign.
active.secondaryText: Because a gem craft without a fixed place stays a hustle. A claim turns it into an establishment.
active.declineText: Studios built later are usually built by somebody else.

stages.completedText: You've already stepped from prospecting into estate-building. That's the right kind of ambition.

config.title: Plot Registrar Helga and the Mason's Claim
config.idleText: Claims move on paper first and lumber second. If I have nothing for you, then either your plot is already filed or you haven't brought me enough intent to be worth the ink.

active.promptText: Stone, lumber, and enough sense to think about a foundation first. That's a decent start. Ready to stake the plot?
active.resultText: Then it's done. You've moved from raw materials to actual property.
active.secondaryText: It means your homestead stops being an idea and starts being a line the town recognizes.
active.declineText: Plots don't admire hesitation.

stages.completedText: Your first claim is already filed. Build something worthy of the paperwork.

config.title: Plot Registrar Helga and Green Acres
config.idleText: Good plots go to people who know what they want to grow there. If I have nothing for you, then your field is already yours or your plan still needs roots.

active.promptText: You brought harvester's proof. Ready to claim the green acres that go with it?
active.resultText: Done. The ground has your name on it now, which means the work gets more interesting from here.
active.secondaryText: Because tending land hits differently once the yield, the mistakes, and the long season all belong to you.
active.declineText: Fields fill with someone else's plans quickly.

stages.completedText: Your field claim is already on file. Make it earn the ink.

config.title: Plot Registrar Helga and the Homesteader's Way
config.idleText: Roofs hold best over people who've already done the ugly work. If I have nothing for you, then the claim is settled or you're still acting like land is decorative.

active.promptText: You helped keep the fields alive. Ready to claim a homestead the hard way instead of the pretty way?
active.resultText: Then the land is yours to prove worthy of.
active.secondaryLabel: What changes once I claim it?
active.secondaryText: Everything. Protection stops being a favor and becomes daily maintenance with your name attached.
active.declineText: Homesteads reward commitment, not flirting.

stages.completedText: Your homestead claim is already settled. Now make it defensible.

config.title: Plot Registrar Helga and Built-to-Last Ground
config.idleText: Builders usually decide faster than this. If I have nothing for you, then the plot is already yours or you still haven't admitted you want permanence.

active.promptText: You can make things that last. Ready to claim land that expects the same of you?
active.resultText: Done. Now you have somewhere permanent enough to justify all that craftsmanship.
active.secondaryLabel: Why does this claim fit a smith?
active.secondaryText: Because good builders eventually need fixed ground, recurring work, and walls that don't move when they look away.
active.declineText: Permanent ground tends to prefer decisive people.

stages.completedText: Your build-site claim is already filed. The rest is labor and standards.

config.title: Plot Registrar Helga and the Farm Apothecary
config.idleText: People always think remedies come after the house. Usually they come before it. If I have nothing for you, then your apothecary claim is already filed or you're still undecided about being useful here.

active.promptText: You can keep workers standing. Ready to claim the kind of plot a proper farm apothecary deserves?
active.resultText: Done. Now people can actually find your remedies instead of hoping they run into you.
active.secondaryText: It turns skill into service with an address, which is usually where livelihoods start.
active.declineText: Remedies without roots stay temporary.

stages.completedText: Your apothecary plot is already registered. Keep the shelves stocked.

config.title: Plot Registrar Helga and a Kitchen of Your Own
config.idleText: A hearth means more once it's yours. If I have nothing for you, then the kitchen claim is already filed or you're still pretending a campfire is enough forever.

active.promptText: Ready to claim a plot big enough for a proper hearth and the kind of kitchen people would come back for?
active.resultText: Done. Every good tavern or farmhouse starts with somebody signing for a hearth.
active.secondaryLabel: Why does a cook need land?
active.secondaryText: Because food gets better when people know where to return for it, and kitchens get better when they stop being temporary.
active.declineText: Hearths don't light themselves.

stages.completedText: Your kitchen plot is already yours. Now make it smell worth crossing the fields for.

config.title: Plot Registrar Helga and Ranch Ground
config.idleText: Ranches start with boundaries and somebody willing to maintain them. If I have nothing for you, then the plot is already yours or you're still treating land like a suggestion.

active.promptText: Ready to claim a plot fit for a leatherworker's workshop and a rancher's patience?
active.resultText: Done. Now you've got the ground to match the gear.
active.secondaryLabel: Why does this fit a leatherworker?
active.secondaryText: Because a ranch is wear, weather, repair, and repetition. Leatherworkers understand all four better than most.
active.declineText: Ranches dislike indecision.

stages.completedText: Your ranch plot is already on file. Make the fences mean something.

config.title: Plot Registrar Helga and the Gem Estate
config.idleText: People laugh at gem estates until the first studio actually starts turning profit. If I have nothing for you, then the claim is settled or your ambition still hasn't caught up to your talent.

active.promptText: Ready to claim the plot that turns your prospecting into a proper estate instead of a lucky streak?
active.resultText: Done. Now you've got land fit for turning rough finds into a real business.
active.secondaryText: Because some ambitions need room, glass, and the confidence to stop pretending they're only temporary.
active.declineText: Estates are usually claimed by people willing to sound ambitious out loud.

stages.completedText: Your estate claim is already filed. Make the place worthy of the title.
